phase_metadata.description:
Mastering the ability of objects to take many
forms through Method Overloading, Overriding, Abstract Classes, and
Interfaces.

learning_objectives:
Understand
the difference between compile-time and runtime polymorphism

Implement method
overloading and overriding correctly

Apply dynamic method dispatch for
flexible code design

Create and implement abstract classes to provide
templates
Design systems using interfaces with default and static methods
Differentiate between abstraction via classes and interfaces
introduction:
Polymorphism is a
Greek word meaning 'many forms'. In Java, it allows one interface or method to
be used for a general class of actions. The specific action is determined by the
exact nature of the situation. It is the third pillar of Object-Oriented
Programming, working hand-in-hand with inheritance to make code flexible and
reusable.

concept_explanation.what_is_it:
Polymorphism allows an
object to be treated as an instance of its parent class or an interface it
implements. This enables one variable to hold different types of objects at
different times.
concept_explanation.types_of_polymorphism:
1. Compile-time Polymorphism
(Static Binding): Achieved through Method Overloading. The compiler determines
which method to call based on the method signature.
2. Runtime Polymorphism
(Dynamic Binding): Achieved through Method Overriding and Dynamic Method
Dispatch. The JVM determines which method to call at runtime based on the actual
object type.
concept_explanation.abstraction:
Abstraction is the process of hiding
implementation details and showing only functionality. In Java, we achieve this
using Abstract Classes (0 to 100% abstraction) and Interfaces (100% abstraction,
though Java 8+ allows default methods).
concept_explanation.why_use_it:
It allows us to write
'generic' code that works with a broad category of objects (like 'Shape') while
allowing specific behaviors (like 'Circle' or 'Square') to be executed
automatically.
concept_explanation.memory_behavior:
During Dynamic Method Dispatch, the JVM
uses a virtual method table (vtable) to look up the actual method implementation
of the object on the Heap, even if the reference variable is of the parent type
on the Stack.

abstract_keyword:
Used to declare a class that cannot be instantiated or a
method that has no body.
interface_keyword:
Used to declare a reference
type that is a collection of abstract methods and constant declarations.
override_annotation:
Tells the compiler that the method is intended to
override a method in a superclass. Helps catch spelling errors.
implements_keyword: Used by a class to adopt an interface.
default_keyword:
Used within an interface to provide a method body, allowing
backward compatibility.
static_interface_methods:
Methods in an interface
that belong to the interface itself and cannot be overridden.
rules_and_key_points:
Constructor and private methods cannot be
overridden.

The return type of an overriding method must be the same or a
subtype (covariant return type).

An abstract class can have constructors,
while an interface cannot.

A class can implement multiple interfaces but
extend only one class.

Static methods are hidden, not overridden, in
inheritance.
Interface variables are implicitly public, static, and final.
Abstract methods must be implemented by the first concrete subclass.

The
@Override annotation is optional but highly recommended for safety.
Polymorphism requires an 'is-a' relationship (inheritance or implementation).
Final methods cannot be overridden, and final classes cannot be extended.

code_example_1.title: Method Overloading and Overriding
code_example_1.description:
Demonstrating how a single method name behaves differently based on parameters
(Static) and object type (Dynamic).

code_example_2.title: Abstract Classes and Interfaces (Java 21)
code_example_2.description:
Using an interface for behavior and an abstract class for a
template in a payment system.

code_example_2.expected_output:
Sum 1: 15
Sum 2: 30
Dog barks
Validating Credit Card for
Visa
Charging $250.0 via Credit Card
Receipt generated for: $250.0
code_example_2.step_by_step_dry_run:
1. Main method starts. 
2. 'calc' object is created.
Compiler links 'add(int, int)' to the first call based on two arguments.
3.
'myPet' reference of type 'Animal' is created on the Stack.
4. A 'Dog' object
is created on the Heap.
5. 'myPet' points to the 'Dog' object. 
6. When
'myPet.speak()' is called, JVM looks at the actual object (Dog) and finds the
overridden 'speak' method.
7. 'Dog barks' is printed. This is Runtime
Polymorphism.
code_example_2.memory_visualization:
Stack: contains reference variables
like 'myPet' (type Animal). 
Heap: contains the actual object 'new Dog()'.

Method Area: contains class templates for Animal and Dog. 
At runtime,
'myPet.speak()' follows the reference to the Heap, checks the object's class
metadata, and executes the version of 'speak' found in the Dog class.
real_world_use_cases:
JDBC Driver: The same 'Connection' interface is used
for MySQL, PostgreSQL, and Oracle databases.

UI Frameworks: A generic
'Button' class where 'onClick' behavior is overridden for specific buttons.

Payment Gateways: Treating PayPal, Stripe, and Credit Cards as a generic
'Payment' type.

File Handling: Treating local files and cloud files via a
common 'Storage' interface.
common_mistakes:
Trying to instantiate an
abstract class using 'new AbstractClass()'.

Forgetting the @Override
annotation, leading to accidental method overloading (mismatching parameters).
Thinking that static methods can be overridden (they are actually hidden).

Not making an overriding method public when the interface method is public by
default.

Confusing 'abstract' with 'interface'—abstract classes allow shared
state (instance variables), interfaces (mostly) don't.
best_practices: Always use @Override to catch signature mismatches at compile time.

Program
to an interface, not an implementation (e.g., List list = new ArrayList()).
Use abstract classes for 'is-a' relationships with shared code.

Use
interfaces for 'can-do' capabilities (e.g., Drawable, Runnable).

Keep
interfaces small and focused (Interface Segregation Principle).

faq.question: Can an interface have a constructor?
faq.answer:
No. Interfaces
cannot be instantiated and do not have instance state that requires
initialization via a constructor.

faq.question:
What is the difference
between Overloading and Overriding?
faq.answer:
Overloading occurs in the same
class with different parameters (compile-time). Overriding occurs in a subclass
with the same signature (runtime).

faq.question:
Can we override a static
method?
faq.answer:
No. Static methods are bound to the class, not the
instance. Defining a static method with the same signature in a subclass is
called 'Method Hiding'.

code_example_2.summary:
Phase 7 introduced Polymorphism and
Abstraction. We learned that polymorphism allows objects to behave differently
based on their actual type at runtime. We explored how abstract classes provide
a partial blueprint while interfaces provide a strict contract for behavior,
including the modern Java features of default and static methods.
key_takeaways:
Overloading is
checked by the compiler; Overriding is checked by the JVM.

Dynamic Method
Dispatch enables loose coupling.

Abstract classes can have state; Interfaces
define pure behavior.

Default methods allow interfaces to evolve without
breaking existing code.
code_example_2.next_topic_preview:
In Phase 8, we will explore
Encapsulation and Packages, learning how to protect data and organize our Java
projects into professional modules.

questions.question:
What is the primary difference between
compile-time and runtime polymorphism in Java?
questions.answer:
Compile-time
polymorphism is achieved through method overloading where the compiler selects
the method based on the signature. Runtime polymorphism is achieved through
method overriding where the JVM selects the method based on the actual object
type at execution time.

questions.question:
When would you prefer an abstract class over an interface in
Java 21?
questions.answer:
Use an abstract class when you want to share common code
(methods with bodies) and state (instance variables) among closely related
classes. Use an interface when you want to define a contract that can be
implemented by unrelated classes, or when you need a class to implement multiple
behaviors.

questions.question:
Write a small code snippet demonstrating 'Upcasting' and explaining
why it is useful.
questions.answer:
Example: 'Shape s = new Circle();'. This is
upcasting. It is useful because it allows us to create methods that accept a
'Shape' parameter, and we can pass any subclass like Circle, Square, or Triangle
to it, making the code more flexible.

questions.question:
Implement an interface named 'SmartDevice' with
an abstract method 'turnOn()' and a default method 'updateFirmware()' that
prints a generic update message. Then, create a class 'SmartPhone' that
implements it.

Identify the bug: 'abstract
class Test { abstract static void display(); }'
questions.answer:
The bug is that a
method cannot be both 'abstract' and 'static'. Abstract methods are meant to be
overridden by subclasses to provide an implementation for an instance, whereas
static methods belong to the class and cannot be overridden.

questions.question:
What happens if a subclass
overrides a method but changes the access modifier from 'public' in the parent
to 'protected' in the child?
questions.answer:
This will cause a compilation error.
In Java, an overriding method cannot assign a weaker access privilege. Since
'public' is more accessible than 'protected', the child class must keep it
'public'.

questions.question:
Create
a class hierarchy: Parent 'Employee' with a method 'getSalary()', and Child
'Developer' that overrides 'getSalary()' to add a bonus.

Demonstrate Dynamic Method Dispatch with three levels of inheritance:
GrandParent, Parent, and Child, each overriding a method 'identify()'. Use a
GrandParent reference to call the method on a Child object.

code_example_2.question:
Can an abstract
class have a main method?

options.A: Yes, and it can be executed
options.B: No, abstract classes cannot have any static methods
options.C:
Yes, but it
cannot be executed
options.D: No, because abstract classes cannot be instantiated

answer.question: Which of these is TRUE about interfaces in Java 8 and later?

options.B:
They can have default methods
with implementation

question.question: Which of the following is NOT required for Method Overriding?
Complete the code: interface Flyable { _____ void fly(); }

options.B:
Defining a static method in a subclass with the same signature as one in the
superclass
options.C: Using the 'final' keyword on a class
options.D:
Encapsulating
methods in a private package

question.question:
Best Approach:
You want to define a behavior for 'Printing' that can be applied to both a
'Document' class and a 'Photo' class. Which should you use?

options.B:
A single class with method
overloading
options.D:
Static methods in a Utility
class
